moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Ware Opening, an unusual first move that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside by advancing our a-pawn. While a4 doesn't control the center like e4 or d4, it prepares to support queenside expansion and prevents Black from playing ...a5 themselves. This move is favored by players who want to avoid mainstream theory and control the game on their own terms—it's unconventional but playable, giving us flexibility to develop our plan while keeping Black guessing about our intentions.

Black mirrors our move symmetrically, which is a principled response that prevents us from gaining space on the queenside. By playing ...a5, Black restricts our a-pawn and locks the a-file, eliminating one of White's potential advantages. This symmetric approach neutralizes our opening idea and suggests Black intends to focus on the center and kingside, where the real battle will be fought—making the opening's first moves essentially a draw in terms of position.
